sections.title: My Favourite Food
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens by inviting children to draw and name their favourite homemade food dishes, establishing a personal connection to food. Students share reasons for their preferences and discuss the diversity of tastes — sweet, sour, spicy, bitter. An IKS element is introduced through the concept of Chhappan Bhog, the traditional festival offering of 56 food items representing six tastes, illustrating India's rich culinary heritage.

tasks.task_text: Draw pictures of your favourite homemade food dishes and name them.
Share your reasons for why you like the food items you have drawn.
Talk to the elders in your family and discuss in the class: Why do we need to eat and drink?

sections.title: Story of Shirin — the Runner
sections.content_summary: Through the story of Shirin, a Class 3 runner who frequently falls sick, the chapter explains that a balanced diet requires variety — vegetables, fruits, multiple grains (rice, ragi, jowar, wheat, bajra), pulses, and nuts — not just a few preferred items. Shirin improves her health and athletic performance after adopting a varied, home-cooked diet, making the link between food diversity and physical well-being concrete and relatable. Students tick food items they eat regularly and identify which ones form a balanced diet.

tasks.task_text: Why do we need to eat healthy food?

sections.title: We Eat Different Things
sections.content_summary: The section highlights how food choices vary by season — cool drinks and juicy fruits in summer, warm soups and stews in winter — and by region across India. Children are encouraged to discover foods from other parts of the country through travel stories and contacts. The section underscores the link between seasonal availability of produce and traditional dietary practices, building appreciation for India's food diversity.

tasks.task_text: Make a list of fruits and vegetables available and special food items that are prepared in the summer and in the winter.

sections.title: Where does food come from?
sections.content_summary: This section categorises food by its origin — different parts of plants (roots: carrot, radish, beetroot; leaves: spinach, fenugreek, cabbage, mustard, coriander; fruits: guava, sapota, apple, mango; stems: potatoes, bamboo shoots, ginger, onion; seeds: grains, pulses, dry fruits) and animal-based foods (milk, curd, ghee, cheese, paneer, honey, eggs, meat). A puzzle prompts students to think of ingredients from neither plants nor animals (salt, water). The section also reinforces the importance of drinking water frequently, especially during physical activity and in summer.

tasks.task_text: Do you know which foods are grown in the fields? Write a few names of such crops.
Discuss and write down in what form some of these foods are cooked.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The reflection section consolidates chapter learning through four sub-sections — Discuss (reasons for food variety, seasonal eating, identifying healthy vs. unhealthy food, reducing food waste), Write (listing family fruits and vegetables, writing a recipe, naming a food with both plant and animal ingredients), Draw (drawing three thalis for morning, afternoon and night meals), an Eating Together activity (making and sharing a fruit chaat), and a Think prompt (planning food for a guest). These tasks together move students from personal experience to cultural and civic food values.

tasks.task_text: Why do we need different kinds of food?
Why do we eat seasonal foods?
How can you tell if a food is healthy or unhealthy?
Why is it important to avoid wasting food? Can you think of some ways to reduce food waste at home?
List the fruits and vegetables eaten in your family.
Write the recipe of your favourite dish.

Write a name of a food item that includes both plant and animal based ingredients. For example, lassi includes yogurt/curd which is prepared from milk (animal based ingredient) and sugar which is prepared from sugarcane (a plant based ingredient).
Draw three plates or thalis with various food items in your notebook.
In these plates draw and label food items that you eat in the morning, afternoon and at night.
Bring any fruit to the class. Prepare a fruit chaat or salad with the help of your teacher. Share it with your friends.
Imagine you suddenly have a guest visiting your home. What kind of food would you serve them and why?

dual_strand.natural: Food as essential nutrition — sources from plants and animals, balanced diet, seasonal produce, hydration
dual_strand.human_cultural: India's diverse food traditions, Chhappan Bhog, regional cuisines, seasonal food practices, hospitality and sharing food
